2017年10月19日，加拿大天文学家罗伯特·韦里克（Robert Weryk）在审查由Pan-望远镜捕获的图像时发现了奇怪的东西。望远镜位于毛伊岛上一万英尺的火山峰哈雷阿卡拉（Haleakalā）顶上，它每晚扫描天空，并使用世界上最高分辨率的相机记录结果。它的设计目的是寻找“近地天体”，这些天体主要是小行星，它们的路径将它们带入我们星球的天文邻域，并且以每小时约四万英里的平均速度行进。引起韦里克注意的光点以每小时近二十万英里的速度移动四倍以上。
 Weryk提醒了同事，后者开始跟踪其他天文台的观测点。他们越看，它的行为就越令人困惑。该物体很小，面积大约是一个城市街区。当它在太空中翻滚时，它的亮度变化很大（十分之一），以至于它必须具有非常奇怪的形状。它既长又瘦，就像宇宙的雪茄，或者又扁又圆，就像天上的比萨饼。它不是沿着椭圆形的路径绕着太阳摆动，而是或多或少地沿直线拉开。天文学家总结说，这个亮点是前所未有的。那是一个“星际物体”，一个远远超出刚刚穿过的太阳系的访客。在国际天文学联盟的干燥术语中，它被称为1I / 2017 U1。更令人回味的是，它被称为夏威夷语中的“ Oumuamua（发音为“ oh-mooah-mooah”），意思是“侦察兵”。
  即使是星际天体也必须遵守重力定律，但‘Oumuamua就像是受到了额外的力量推动一样。彗星由于它们散发出的气体而形成了一个附加的脚步，形成了它们的标志性尾巴。 ‘但是，Oumuamua没有尾巴。受过训练的望远镜也没有找到通常与除气有关的任何副产物的证据，例如水蒸气或灰尘。
  观察者制作的视频“这绝对是一个不寻常的物体”。 “而且，不幸的是，不可能再有新的观察到'Oumuamua，因为它已经太暗了，太远了。”
 随着天文学家对数据的深入研究，他们排除了一种理论。 “ Oumuamua怪异的运动不能通过与另一个物体的碰撞，与太阳风的相互作用或在19世纪波兰工程师之后被称为Yarkovsky效应的现象来解释。一组研究人员认为，最好的解释是1I / 2017 U1是“微型彗星”，其尾巴由于其“异常的化学成分”而未被发现。另一个小组认为，‘Oumuamua主要由冷冻氢组成。这个假设是对微型彗星概念的一种变体，它具有解释物体特殊形状的优点。当它到达我们的太阳系时，它几乎已经融化了，就像人行道上的冰块一样。
  迄今为止，II / 2017 U1最引人注目的描述来自哈佛天体物理学家Avi Loeb。勒布认为，“ Oumuamua的表现不会像星际物体那样，因为它不是一个。这是外星文明的产物。
 在韦里克被发现一年后，这封信出现在《天体物理学杂志》上。它可能相当于废弃汽车的异物，“漂浮在星际空间中”为“碎片”。或者可能是已派遣到我们的太阳系进行侦察的“一个全面运行的探测器”。勒布（Leeb）和比亚利（Bialy）提出的第二种可能性是可能性更高，因为如果该物体只是一块外星垃圾，在银河系中漂流，那么我们碰到它的几率就非常低。勒布在《科学美国人》（Scientific American）博客上写道。 
毫不奇怪，勒布和比亚利的理论受到了很多关注。这个故事几乎以'Oumuamua的速度在世界范围内传播。电视人员挤到哈佛大学史密森天体物理中心Loeb的办公室，并出现在他家中。电影公司争相拍摄他一生的电影。同样也不足为奇的是，很多注意力都令人欣喜。
 俄亥俄州立大学天体物理学家保罗·萨特（Paul S.Sutter）写道：``不，‘Oumuamua不是外星飞船，该论文的作者侮辱了诚实的科学询问甚至提出了建议。''
 “我们能谈谈阿维·勒布（Avi Loeb）提倡关于'Oumuamua外星人起源的投机理论是多么令人讨厌，迫使我们其余的人为回避这些谣言做出科学的努力吗？”亚利桑那大学的天文学家本杰明·韦纳（Benjamin Weiner）发了推文。
  勒布并没有受到阻吓，反而加倍了。他与韩国天文学与太空科学研究所的研究员Thiem Hoang一起爆炸了冷冻氢理论。在另一篇充满方程式的论文中，两人认为想象固体氢在外层空间周围漂浮是不可思议的。而且，如果冰冻的冰块确实能够成型，那么“ Oumuamua”大小的冰块就无法在星际旅行中生存。 Hoang和Loeb写道：“假设可以以某种方式形成H 2物体，”通过碰撞加热升华”会在它们有机会以某种方式起飞之前将其汽化。
 勒布现在已经放弃了科学记号，并写了“外星人：超越地球的智能生命的第一个迹象”（霍顿·米夫林·哈考特）。在书中，他讲述了一个常说的故事，即伽利略如何因宣称地球绕太阳旋转而被指控异端。 1633年，伽利略（Galileo）在罗马受审时退缩，然后传说中喃喃自语地说“ Eppur si muove”（“却动了”）。勒布承认报价可能是伪造的。他坚持认为，这仍然很重要。天文机构可能希望让他保持沉默，但这无法解释为什么“ Oumuamua偏离了预期路径。他观察到：“但是它偏离了。”
 勒布在《外星人》中阐述了他的推理如下。在不诉诸某种无法察觉的除气的情况下，理解“ Oumuamua”奇怪加速的唯一方法是，假设该物体是由太阳辐射推动的—本质上是光子从其表面反弹。而且，只有极低的密度和相对较大的表面积，它才能被太阳辐射推动，唯一的方法就是它非常薄（不比毫米厚）。这样的物体将起帆的作用-由光而不是风驱动。自然界不会产生帆；人们做。因此，勒布写道：``‘Oumuamua必须是由外星情报机构设计，建造和发射的。”
  1995年，一对瑞士天文学家，米歇尔·马约尔（Michel Mayor）和迪迪埃·奎洛兹（Didier Queloz）发现了第一个绕太阳星旋转的行星。它的寄宿星51 Pegasi位于飞马座，因此该行星被正式称为51 Pegasi b。通过不同的命名约定，它被称为Dimidium。 
Dimidium是当时的“ Oumuamua”，这是一个了不起的发现，在世界各地引起头条新闻。 （由于他们的工作，市长和奎洛兹最终被授予诺贝尔奖。）这颗行星非常大，质量约为地球的150倍。它每四天绕着它的恒星鞭打一次，这意味着它必须相对靠近它，并且可能非常热，其表面温度高达1800度。天文学家没有想到能找到如此大的恒星离它的母恒星那么近，而不得不发明一个全新的类别来容纳它。它被称为“热木星”。
 市长和奎洛兹通过在51贝加西（Pegasi）上测量引力拉力来检测出Di。 2009年，发射了开普勒太空望远镜，该望远镜旨在使用其他方法搜索系外行星。当行星经过恒星前方时，它会非常轻微地降低恒星的亮度。 （在2012年金星的最后一次飞行期间，地球上的观众可以看到一个小黑点在太阳上爬行。）开普勒测量了天鹅座和天琴座附近超过十五万颗恒星的亮度变化。到2015年，它已经揭示出一千颗系外行星的存在。到2018年停止运营时，它已经揭示了1600多个。
  望远镜的最终目标是计算出一个称为eta-Earth（η-地球）的数字。这是可以发现的，大致与地球一样大小的岩石状行星的平均数量，该行星围绕一个普通的太阳般恒星围绕一定距离，这很可能使它们适应居住。在花了两年时间分析开普勒的数据之后，研究人员最近得出结论，η⊕的值介于0.37至0.6之间。由于银河系中至少有40亿颗类似太阳的恒星，这意味着从理论上讲，我们银河系中大约15亿至24亿个行星可以掩盖生命。没有人知道实际上有多少可居住的星球有人居住，但是，即使比例很小，我们仍在谈论银河系中数以百万计（也许是数千万）的可能充满生物的行星。在几年前的一次公开活动中，当时是当时的首席科学家，现为国家航空航天博物馆馆长的埃伦·斯托凡（Ellen Stofan）说，她相信“超越地球的生命”的“确定性证据”将是在未来二十年的某个时间发现。
 天体物理学家杰弗里·纽马克（Jeffrey Newmark）在同一聚会上说：“这绝对不是'如果'，而是'时间'。”
  如果找到（如果不是），其他星球上的生命将是什么样？剑桥大学的研究员Arik Kershenbaum在“动物学家的银河指南：地球上的哪些动物揭示了外星人和我们自己”中解决了这个问题（企鹅出版社）。他写道：“人们普遍认为，外星人的生活太过陌生了，无法想象。” “我不同意。”
 Kershenbaum认为，了解宇宙生态学的关键是自然选择。他认为，这是生命发展的“必然机制”，因此，它“不仅限于地球”，甚至不限于碳基生物。无论外来生物化学如何起作用，“自然选择将在其背后”。
 克申鲍姆说，从这个前提出发，可以得出结论，其他行星上的生命将会演化，如果不是沿着与这个星球上生命相同的路线发展，那么至少会沿着通常可以识别的路线发展。例如，在地球上，大气主要由氮和氧构成，羽毛是有用的功能。在一个由氨气制成的云层的星球上，羽毛可能不会出现，“但我们在这里看到的功能（即飞行）不会感到惊讶。”同样，Kershenbaum写道，外来生物易于进化出某种形式的陆上运动-“外星星球上的生命很可能有腿”，以及类似于性的某种复制形式和某种信息交换方式：黑暗中的外星人会像蝙蝠和海豚一样咔嗒作响，而晴朗的天空中的外星人则会彼此闪烁色彩。” 
假设实际上那里有外星生命，那么大多数似乎都是微观的。当斯托芬说我们很快就会找到它时，“我们不是在谈论小矮人”。 “我们正在谈论小微生物。”但是研究动物传播的Kershenbaum直接跳到复杂的生物体上，这很快就使他进入了Loebian领土。
 在地球上，许多动物都拥有我们广泛称为“智能”的东西。克森鲍姆（Kershenbaum）认为，鉴于这种品质所带来的优势，整个银河系的自然选择将有利于它的出现，在这种情况下，应该有许多像我们一样聪明的生命形式，还有一些是整体的生命形式。更聪明。在他看来，这打开了一大罐星际蠕虫。我们要给外星人“人权”吗？他们会赋予我们任何权利，如果有的话，授予他们小的绿色（或银色或蓝色）弟兄？克尔森鲍姆（Kershenbaum）承认，这样的问题很难事先回答，“没有任何证据表明外星人自己可能拥有什么样的法律制度或道德制度。”
  就像遇到聪明的外星人一样令人不安，我们尚未得到任何消息的事实，甚至可以说更多。为什么会出现这种情况，这个问题被称为费米悖论。
 1950年的一天，物理学家恩里科·费米（Enrico Fermi）在洛斯阿拉莫斯国家实验室（Los Alamos National Laboratory）午餐时，转向一些同事问道：“他们在哪里？” （至少，这是故事的一个版本；根据另一个版本，他问：“但是，每个人都在哪里？”）这是Pan-1和开普勒任务之前的几十年。费米仍然认为，地球是围绕一颗相当典型的恒星旋转的一颗相当典型的行星。他认为，那里应该有比我们自己更古老，更先进的文明，其中一些文明本应该已经掌握了星际旅行。然而，奇怪的是，没有人出现。
  此后，许多人类情报都致力于解决费米的问题。在十九世纪六十年代，一位名叫弗兰克·德雷克（Frank Drake）的天文学家提出了同名的德雷克方程式，该方程式提供了一种估算方式，或者，如果您愿意的话，可以估算出我们可能希望与之交流的几种外来文化。等式中的关键术语包括：在那里有多少潜在宜居的行星，将有多少生命的行星将发展尖端技术，以及尖端技术文明将持续多久。随着潜在宜居行星的增加，“它们在哪里？”谜底只会加深。在2019年于巴黎举行的关于这个主题的研讨会上，法国研究人员让·皮埃尔·罗斯帕斯（Jean-Pierre Rospars）提出建议，认为外星人没有与我们联系，因为他们将地球置于“银隔离”之下。他说，他们意识到，“对我们而言，了解他们在文化上将具有破坏性。”
 勒布（Loeb）提出费米可能是他自己悖论的答案。在过去的一百多年中，人类一直能够通过无线电波与其他行星通信。 75年前，费米和他在曼哈顿计划中的同事发明了原子弹，几年后，费米的同伴之一在爱德华·泰勒（Lod Alamos）在洛斯阿拉莫斯的午餐桌上提出了氢弹的设计。因此，在人类有能力向其他星球发出信号后不久，它也有能力消灭自己。自从核武器发明以来，我们一直在想出新的方法来从事自己的工作；其中包括不受控制的气候变化和人造微生物。
 勒布警告说：“完全可以想象，如果我们不小心，文明的下一个世纪将是它的最后一个世纪。”他推断，“具有探索宇宙的技术能力”的外来文明同样“很容易因自残而被歼灭”。也许没有人出现的原因是没有人去旅行。这将意味着‘Oumuamua等同于陶器的宇宙观-一种已经死去的文化产物。 
地球人可能会从这种（公认的高度投机性）思想中得到的信息是：警惕新技术。勒布（Loeb）得出相反的结论。他认为人类应该努力生产出他想象中的那种“光子动力船”。为此，他是一个名为“突破性星散计划”（Breakthrough Starshot Initiative）的项目的顾问，该计划的宗旨是“展示超快光驱动纳米技术的概念证明。”从长远来看，该组织希望为发射到距离地球约25万亿英里的最接近地球的恒星系统Alpha Centauri奠定“基础”。 （该计划获得了俄罗斯-以色列亿万富翁尤里•米尔纳（Yuri Milner）的资助，其成员包括马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）。
 勒布还期待有一天我们能够“在实验室中创造合成生活”。从那里，他想到了“古登堡DNA打印机”，可以“分发来从其他行星表面的原材料中复制人类基因组”。他建议，通过用我们的遗传物质为银河系播种，我们可以对付灭绝的赌注。我们还可以进行一项伟大的进化实验，该实验可能会导致结果比迄今为止所看到的更加奇妙。勒布写道：“没有理由期望在地球上随机环境下出现的陆地生命是最佳的。”
  小时候，我最喜欢的书之一是埃里希·冯·丹尼克（Erich vonDäniken）撰写的《众神的战车》。罗德·瑟林（Rod Serling）讲述了这本书的前提，并把它分解成电视纪录片“寻找古代宇航员”，是因为费米的问题早就得到了回答。 “他们”已经来过这里。瑞士酒店经理冯·丹尼克（VonDäniken）转为作家，他在纪录片中由于某种原因被描述为德国教授，他辩称外星人曾在迷雾笼罩的过去某个时候降落在地球上。在传说中以及秘鲁南部的纳斯卡线等文物中都记录了他们访问的踪迹。人们为什么要创建这些过大的图像，而不是向空中的生物发出信号？
 我认为冯·丹尼肯会对第一个正式的星际物体感兴趣，因此我与他取得了联系。现年八十五岁的他住在因特拉肯（Interlaken）附近，距离他设计的主题公园不远，该主题公园最初被称为神秘公园（Mystery Park），后来在一系列金融灾难后更名为少女峰公园（Jungfrau Park）。公园拥有七个凉亭，一个凉亭形状像金字塔，另一个凉亭像阿兹台克神庙。
 冯·丹尼克（VonDäniken）告诉我，的确，他一直在关注有关“乌玛穆阿（Oumuamua）”的争议。他倾向于与勒布站在一边，他认为他很勇敢。
 他说：“他需要勇气，显然他有勇气。” “没有科学家愿意被嘲笑，每当他们与U.F.O.或外星人打交道时，他们都会被媒体嘲笑。”但是，他预测，“情况将会改变。”
  人们常说“非同寻常的主张需要非凡的证据。”这个词是由天文学家卡尔·萨根（Carl Sagan）推广的，他可能做出了与任何科学家一样的努力来促进寻找外星生命的努力。莱布的主张有时被称为“萨根标准”，显然没有兑现；他为“ Oumuamua是一种外星飞船”的理论封送了最好的证据，那就是替代性理论令人信服。勒布（Loeb）明确拒绝了萨根（Sagan）标准：“对我而言，为什么非同寻常的主张为什么需要非同寻常的证据，”他观察到-并颠倒了它的逻辑：“非同寻常的保守主义使我们格外无知。”只要有机会1I / 2017 U1被外星人探测，我们就愚蠢不采取这个想法。他写道：“如果我们承认‘Oumuamua貌似是外星技术的起源，那么我们将发现全新的证据和发现探索视野。” 
在发表他的理论时，勒布肯定有冒险（并遭受）嘲笑的危险。 冯·达尼肯（vonDäniken）的作品比“伽利略”（Galileo）的作品更可能被评为“外星人”。 尽管如此，正如Serling在“寻找古代宇航员”的结尾指出的那样，令人惊奇地想到了各种可能性：“在晴朗，星光灿烂的夜晚仰望天空，让自己有好奇的自由。” ♦
note_en:
On October 19, 2017, a Canadian astronomer named Robert Weryk was reviewing images captured by a telescope known as Pan-  when he noticed something strange. The telescope is situated atop Haleakalā, a ten-thousand-foot volcanic peak on the island of Maui, and it scans the sky each night, recording the results with the world’s highest-definition camera. It’s designed to hunt for “near-Earth objects,” which are mostly asteroids whose paths bring them into our planet’s astronomical neighborhood and which travel at an average velocity of some forty thousand miles an hour. The dot of light that caught Weryk’s attention was moving more than four times that speed, at almost two hundred thousand miles per hour.
 Weryk alerted colleagues, who began tracking the dot from other observatories. The more they looked, the more puzzling its behavior seemed. The object was small, with an area roughly that of a city block. As it tumbled through space, its brightness varied so much—by a factor of ten—that it had to have a very odd shape. Either it was long and skinny, like a cosmic cigar, or flat and round, like a celestial pizza. Instead of swinging around the sun on an elliptical path, it was zipping away more or less in a straight line. The bright dot, astronomers concluded, was something never before seen. It was an “interstellar object”—a visitor from far beyond the solar system that was just passing through. In the dry nomenclature of the International Astronomical Union, it became known as 1I/2017 U1. More evocatively, it was dubbed ‘Oumuamua (pronounced “oh-mooah-mooah”), from the Hawaiian, meaning, roughly, “scout.”
  Even interstellar objects have to obey the law of gravity, but ‘Oumuamua raced along as if propelled by an extra force. Comets get an added kick thanks to the gases they throw off, which form their signature tails. ‘Oumuamua, though, didn’t have a tail. Nor did the telescopes trained on it find evidence of any of the by-products normally associated with outgassing, like water vapor or dust.
  “This is definitely an unusual object,” a video produced by   observed. “And, unfortunately, no more new observations of ‘Oumuamua are possible because it’s already too dim and far away.”
 As astronomers pored over the data, they excluded one theory after another. ‘Oumuamua’s weird motion couldn’t be accounted for by a collision with another object, or by interactions with the solar wind, or by a phenomenon that’s known, after a nineteenth-century Polish engineer, as the Yarkovsky effect. One group of researchers decided that the best explanation was that 1I/2017 U1 was a “miniature comet” whose tail had gone undetected because of its “unusual chemical composition.” Another group argued that ‘Oumuamua was composed mostly of frozen hydrogen. This hypothesis—a variation on the mini-comet idea—had the advantage of explaining the object’s peculiar shape. By the time it reached our solar system, it had mostly melted away, like an ice cube on the sidewalk.
  By far the most spectacular account of 1I/2017 U1 came from  Avi Loeb, a Harvard astrophysicist. ‘Oumuamua didn’t behave as an interstellar object would be expected to, Loeb argued, because it wasn’t one. It was the handiwork of an alien civilization.
 In an equation-dense paper that appeared in  The  Astrophysical Journal Letters a year after Weryk’s discovery, Loeb and a Harvard postdoc named Shmuel Bialy proposed that ‘Oumuamua’s “non-gravitational acceleration” was most economically explained by assuming that the object was manufactured. It might be the alien equivalent of an abandoned car, “floating in interstellar space” as “debris.” Or it might be “a fully operational probe” that had been dispatched to our solar system to reconnoitre. The second possibility, Loeb and Bialy suggested, was the more likely, since if the object was just a piece of alien junk, drifting through the galaxy, the odds of our having come across it would be absurdly low. “In contemplating the possibility of an artificial origin, we should keep in mind what Sherlock Holmes said: ‘when you have excluded the impossible, whatever remains, however improbable, must be the truth,’ ” Loeb  wrote in a blog post for  Scientific American.
  Not surprisingly, Loeb and Bialy’s theory received a lot of attention. The story raced around the world almost at the speed of ‘Oumuamua. TV crews crowded into Loeb’s office, at the Harvard-Smithsonian Center for Astrophysics, and showed up at his house. Film companies vied to make a movie of his life. Also not surprisingly, much of the attention was unflattering.
 “No, ‘Oumuamua is not an alien spaceship, and the authors of the paper insult honest scientific inquiry to even suggest it,” Paul M. Sutter, an astrophysicist at Ohio State University, wrote.
 “Can we talk about how annoying it is that Avi Loeb promotes speculative theories about alien origins of ‘Oumuamua, forcing [the] rest of us to do the scientific gruntwork of walking back these rumors?” Benjamin Weiner, an astronomer at the University of Arizona, tweeted.
  Far from being deterred, Loeb doubled down. Together with Thiem Hoang, a researcher at the Korea Astronomy and Space Science Institute, he blasted the frozen-hydrogen theory. In another equation-packed paper, the pair argued that it was fantastical to imagine solid hydrogen floating around outer space. And, if a frozen chunk did manage to take shape, there was no way for a block the size of ‘Oumuamua to survive an interstellar journey. “Assuming that H 2 objects could somehow form,” Hoang and Loeb wrote, “sublimation by collisional heating” would vaporize them before they had the chance to, in a manner of speaking, take off.
 Loeb has now dispensed with the scientific notation and written “ Extraterrestrial: The First Sign of Intelligent Life Beyond Earth” (Houghton Mifflin Harcourt). In it, he recounts the oft-told story of how Galileo was charged with heresy for asserting that Earth circled the sun. At his trial in Rome, in 1633,  Galileo recanted and then, legend has it, muttered, sotto voce, “ Eppur si muove” (“And yet it moves”). Loeb acknowledges that the quote is probably apocryphal; still, he maintains, it’s relevant. The astronomical establishment may wish to silence him, but it can’t explain why ‘Oumuamua strayed from the expected path. “And yet it deviated,” he observes.
 In “Extraterrestrial,” Loeb lays out his reasoning as follows. The only way to make sense of ‘Oumuamua’s strange acceleration, without resorting to some sort of undetectable outgassing, is to assume that the object was propelled by solar radiation—essentially, photons bouncing off its surface. And the only way the object could be propelled by solar radiation is if it were extremely thin—no thicker than a millimetre—with a very low density and a comparatively large surface area. Such an object would function as a sail—one powered by light, rather than by wind. The natural world doesn’t produce sails; people do. Thus, Loeb writes, “ ‘Oumuamua must have been designed, built, and launched by an extraterrestrial intelligence.”
  The first planet to be found circling a sunlike star was spotted in 1995 by a pair of Swiss astronomers, Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz. Its host star, 51 Pegasi, was in the constellation Pegasus, and so the planet was formally dubbed 51 Pegasi b. By a different naming convention, it became known as Dimidium.
 Dimidium was the ‘Oumuamua of its day—a fantastic discovery that made headlines around the world. (For their work, Mayor and Queloz were eventually awarded a Nobel Prize.) The planet turned out to be very large, with a mass about a hundred and fifty times that of Earth. It was whipping around its star once every four days, which meant that it had to be relatively close to it and was probably very hot, with a surface temperature of as much as eighteen hundred degrees. Astronomers hadn’t thought such a large body could be found so close to its parent star and had to invent a whole new category to contain it; it became known as a “hot Jupiter.”
 Mayor and Queloz had detected Dimidium by measuring its gravitational tug on 51 Pegasi. In 2009,   launched the Kepler space telescope, which was designed to search for exoplanets using a different method. When a planet passes in front of its star, it reduces the star’s brightness very slightly. (During the last transit of Venus, in 2012, viewers on Earth could watch a small black dot creep across the sun.) Kepler measured variations in the brightness of more than a hundred and fifty thousand stars in the vicinity of the constellations Cygnus and Lyra. By 2015, it had revealed the existence of a thousand exoplanets. By the time it stopped operating, in 2018, it had revealed sixteen hundred more.
  ’s ultimate goal for the telescope was to work out a figure known as eta-Earth, or η⊕. This is the average number of rocky, roughly Earth-size planets that can be found orbiting an average sunlike star at a distance that might, conceivably, render them habitable. After spending two years analyzing the data from Kepler, researchers recently concluded that η⊕ has a value somewhere between .37 and .6. Since there are at least four billion sunlike stars in the Milky Way, this means that somewhere between 1.5 billion and 2.4 billion planets in our galaxy could, in theory, harbor life. No one knows what fraction of potentially habitable planets are, in fact, inhabited, but, even if the proportion is trivial, we’re still talking about millions—perhaps tens of millions—of planets in the galaxy that might be teeming with living things. At a public event a few years ago, Ellen Stofan, who at the time was  ’s chief scientist and is now the director of the National Air and Space Museum, said that she believed “definitive evidence” of “life beyond earth” would be found sometime in the next two decades.
 “It’s definitely not an ‘if,’ it’s a ‘when,’ ” Jeffrey Newmark, a   astrophysicist, said at the same gathering.
  What will life on other planets look like, when—not if—it’s found? Arik Kershenbaum, a researcher at the University of Cambridge, takes up this question in “ The Zoologist’s Guide to the Galaxy: What Animals on Earth Reveal About Aliens—and Ourselves” (Penguin Press). “It’s a popular belief that alien life is too alien to imagine,” he writes. “I don’t agree.”
 Kershenbaum argues that the key to understanding cosmic zoology is natural selection. This, he maintains, is the “inevitable mechanism” by which life develops, and therefore it’s “not just restricted to the planet Earth” or even to carbon-based organisms. However alien biochemistry functions, “natural selection will be behind it.”
 From this premise, Kershenbaum says, it follows that life on other planets will have evolved, if not along the same lines as life on this planet, then at least along lines that are generally recognizable. On Earth, for instance, where the atmosphere is mostly made of nitrogen and oxygen, feathers are a useful feature. On a planet where clouds are made of ammonia, feathers probably wouldn’t emerge, “but we should not be surprised to find the same functions (i.e. flight) that we observe here.” Similarly, Kershenbaum writes, alien organisms are apt to evolve some form of land-based locomotion—“Life on alien planets is very likely to have legs”—as well as some form of reproduction analogous to sex and some way of exchanging information: “Aliens in the dark will click like bats and dolphins, and aliens in the clear skies will flash their colours at each other.”
 Assuming that there is, in fact, alien life out there, most of it seems likely to be microscopic. “We are not talking about little green men” is how Stofan put it when she said we were soon going to find it. “We are talking about little microbes.” But Kershenbaum, who studies animal communication, jumps straight to complex organisms, which propels him pretty quickly into Loebian territory.
 On Earth, many animals possess what we would broadly refer to as “intelligence.” Kershenbaum argues that, given the advantages that this quality confers, natural selection all across the galaxy will favor its emergence, in which case there should be loads of life-forms out there that are as smart as we are, and some that are a whole lot smarter. This, in his view, opens up quite a can of interstellar worms. Are we going to accord aliens “human rights”? Will they accord us whatever rights, if any, they grant their little green (or silver or blue) brethren? Such questions, Kershenbaum acknowledges, are difficult to answer in advance, “without any evidence of what kind of legal system or system of ethics the aliens themselves might have.”
  As disconcerting as encountering intelligent aliens would be, the fact that we haven’t yet heard from any is, arguably, even more so. Why this is the case is a question that’s become known as the Fermi paradox.
 One day in 1950, while lunching at Los Alamos National Laboratory, the physicist Enrico Fermi turned to some colleagues and asked, “Where are they?” (At least, this is how one version of the story goes; according to another version, he asked, “But where is everybody?”) This was decades before Pan- 1 and the Kepler mission. Still, Fermi reckoned that Earth was a fairly typical planet revolving around a fairly typical star. There ought, he reasoned, to be civilizations out there far older and more advanced than our own, some of which should have already mastered interstellar travel. Yet, strangely enough, no one had shown up.
  Much human intelligence has since been devoted to grappling with Fermi’s question. In the nineteen-sixties, an astronomer named Frank Drake came up with the eponymous Drake equation, which offers a way to estimate—or, if you prefer, guesstimate—how many alien cultures exist with which we might hope to communicate. Key terms in the equation include: how many potentially habitable planets are out there, what fraction of life-hosting planets will develop sophisticated technology, and how long technologically sophisticated civilizations endure. As the list of potentially habitable planets has grown, the “Where are they?” mystery has only deepened. At a workshop on the subject held in Paris in 2019, a French researcher named Jean-Pierre Rospars proposed that aliens haven’t reached out to us because they’re keeping Earth under a “galactic quarantine.” They realize, he said, that “it would be culturally disruptive for us to learn about them.”
 Loeb proposes that Fermi may be the answer to his own paradox. Humanity has been capable of communicating with other planets, via radio wave, for only the past hundred years or so. Seventy-five years ago, Fermi and his colleagues on the Manhattan Project invented the atomic bomb, and a few years after that Edward Teller, one of Fermi’s companions at the lunch table at Los Alamos, came up with the design for a hydrogen bomb. Thus, not long after humanity became capable of signalling to other planets, it also became capable of wiping itself out. Since the invention of nuclear weapons, we’ve continued to come up with new ways to do ourselves in; these include unchecked climate change and manufactured microbes.
 “It is quite conceivable that if we are not careful, our civilization’s next few centuries will be its last,” Loeb warns. Alien civilizations “with the technological prowess to explore the universe” are, he infers, similarly “vulnerable to annihilation by self-inflicted wounds.” Perhaps the reason no one has shown up is that there’s no one left to make the trip. This would mean that ‘Oumuamua was the cosmic equivalent of a potsherd—the product of a culture now dead.
 A message an earthling might take from this (admittedly highly speculative) train of thought is: be wary of new technologies. Loeb, for his part, draws the opposite conclusion. He thinks humanity ought to be working to produce precisely the kind of photon-powered vessel that he imagines ‘Oumuamua to be. To this end, he’s an adviser on a project called the Breakthrough Starshot Initiative, whose stated aim is to “demonstrate proof of concept for ultra-fast light-driven nanocrafts.” In the longer term, the group hopes to “lay the foundations” for a launch to Alpha Centauri, the star system closest to Earth, which is about twenty-five trillion miles away. (The initiative has funding from Yuri Milner, a Russian-Israeli billionaire, and counts among its board members Mark Zuckerberg.)
 Loeb also looks forward to the day when we’ll be able to “produce synthetic life in our laboratories.” From there, he imagines “Gutenberg DNA printers” that could be “distributed to make copies of the human genome out of raw materials on the surface of other planets.” By seeding the galaxy with our genetic material, we could, he suggests, hedge our bets against annihilation. We could also run a great evolutionary experiment, one that might lead to outcomes far more wondrous than seen so far. “There is no reason to expect that terrestrial life, which emerged under random circumstances on Earth, was optimal,” Loeb writes.
  When I was a kid, one of my favorite books was “ Chariots of the Gods?,” by Erich von Däniken. The premise of the book, which was spun off into the TV documentary “In Search of Ancient Astronauts,” narrated by Rod Serling, was that Fermi’s question had long ago been answered. “They” had already been here. Von Däniken, a Swiss hotel manager turned author who for some reason in the documentary was described as a German professor, argued that aliens had landed on Earth sometime in the misty past. Traces of their visits were recorded in legends and also in artifacts like the Nazca Lines, in southern Peru. Why had people created these oversized images if not to signal to beings in the air?
 I figured that von Däniken would be interested in the first official interstellar object, and so I got in touch with him. Now eighty-five, he lives near Interlaken, not far from a theme park he designed, which was originally called Mystery Park and then later, after a series of financial mishaps, rebranded as Jungfrau Park. The park boasts seven pavilions, one shaped like a pyramid, another like an Aztec temple.
 Von Däniken told me that he had, indeed, been following the controversy over ‘Oumuamua. He tended to side with Loeb, who, he thought, was very brave.
 “He needs courage and obviously he had courage,” he said. “No scientist wants to be ridiculed, and whenever they deal with U.F.O.s or extraterrestrials, they are ridiculed by the media.” But, he predicted, “the situation will change.”
  It’s often said that “extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence.” The phrase was popularized by the astronomer Carl Sagan, who probably did as much as any scientist has done to promote the search for extraterrestrial life. By what’s sometimes referred to as the “Sagan standard,” Loeb’s claim clearly falls short; the best evidence he marshals for his theory that ‘Oumuamua is an alien craft is that the alternative theories are unconvincing. Loeb, though, explicitly rejects the Sagan standard—“It is not obvious to me why extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence,” he observes—and flips its logic on its head: “Extraordinary conservatism keeps us extraordinarily ignorant.” So long as there’s a chance that 1I/2017 U1 is an alien probe, we’d be fools not to pursue the idea. “If we acknowledge that ‘Oumuamua is plausibly of extraterrestrial-technology origin,” he writes, “whole new vistas of exploration for evidence and discovery open before us.”
 In publishing his theory, Loeb has certainly risked (and suffered) ridicule. It seems a good deal more likely that “Extraterrestrial” will be ranked with von Däniken’s work than with Galileo’s. Still, as Serling notes toward the end of “In Search of Ancient Astronauts,” it’s thrilling to imagine the possibilities: “Look up into the sky some clear, starlit night and allow yourself the freedom to wonder.” ♦
title: 我们已经被外星人拜访吗？
title_en: Have We Already Been Visited by Aliens?

华盛顿 - 本周二，联邦上诉法院否决了特朗普政府的计划放宽对发电厂的温室气体排放的限制，从而为总统当选人拜登的方式颁布有关电厂新的，更强的限制。
 美国哥伦比亚特区上诉法院称，特朗普政府的“可负担的清洁能源”规则是对美国环境法的“根本性误解”，这是通过对法律法规的“一系列严峻误读”造成的。
 在特朗普总统任期的最后一整天，它有效地结束了环境保护署削弱和破坏气候变化政策的努力，并终结了一系列惨淡的失败，在这些失败中，法院一再推翻了放松管制的措施。专家们广泛地描述了E.P.A.的连败是现代历史上最糟糕的法律记录之一。
 上诉法院没有恢复2015年关于巴拉克·奥巴马（Barack Obama）总统的E.P.A.的规定。该法案已经颁布，这将迫使公用事业从煤炭转向使用可再生能源以减少排放。但它拒绝了特朗普政府试图废除该规则并将其替换为无牙规则的尝试。
 法官们剔除了特朗普政府的核心论点：解释1970年《清洁空气法案》的唯一可能方法是，联邦政府无权对排放设置国家限制，或迫使各州摆脱化石燃料发电。这种论点本来可以阻止拜登先生或任何未来的政府在没有国会明确的新法律的情况下解决发电厂的气候变化问题。
 法官们说，特朗普政府“可能不会通过想象法规明文没有明确要求的新限制来推卸责任。”
 E.P.A.的女发言人莫莉·布洛克（Molly Block）表示，特朗普政府对该裁定感到失望，并表示“有可能在国家需要监管稳定之际注入更多不确定性”。 
环保组织和法律专家将这一决定证明了政府确实有权应对气候变化这一论点。
 哈佛大学环境法学教授乔迪·弗里曼（Jody Freeman）说：“这是一次巨大的胜利。”
  拜登（Biden）竞选活动的核心承诺是，到2035年消除电力部门的化石燃料排放。根据周二的裁决，拜登（Biden）政府在决定是否使用方式之前，不必等到针对特朗普规则的法律斗争结束弗里曼女士说​​，应对气候变化的法规。她说，相反，拜登E.P.A.可以立即“进攻”。
 弗里曼女士说​​：“真正的胜利是，特朗普政府未能束缚拜登团队的双手。” “他们想锁定狭义的法律解释，使新一届政府无法为电厂设定雄心勃勃的标准。那是他们的整体策略。结果失败了。”
 2015年，奥巴马政府颁布了《清洁电力计划》，该计划的目标是到2030年将电力部门的排放量与2005年的水平相比减少32％。为此，它指示每个州制定计划，逐步淘汰煤炭并增加可再生能源的产生，以消除发电厂的碳排放。
 该措施从未生效。最高法院在2016年表示，在保守派州的一系列诉讼和煤炭行业得到解决之前，各州不必遵守。特朗普总统选举，他E.P.A.后不久废除了《清洁能源计划》。
  E.P.A.即将离任的管理员Andrew Wheeler一位前煤炭游说者用较弱的“负担得起的清洁能源”规则代替了该计划，该规则坚持认为，该法律仅允许该机构制定指导方针，以提高效率或升级不会威胁整个电力的锅炉等行动来减少单个发电厂的排放。部门，例如煤炭。 
倡导碳税的保守组织市场解决方案联盟执行董事亚历克斯·弗林特（Alex Flint）说，该裁决给发电厂，钢铁厂，水泥窑和其他污染行业的所有者造成了“混乱”。
 “一个主管部门将规则推向一个方向，而下一主管部门将它们推向另一个方向。然后，法院放弃了这些规则。做出有效的长期决策是不可能的。”他说。
 他呼吁国会为排放定价，“以便污染者可以决定是否继续运营并支付运营成本或改变运营方式。”
 弗里曼女士说​​，政府可能会在接下来的24小时内尝试提交一些最新的文件，但是他们获得成功的机会非常低。
 拜登是否会寻求再次利用法规来限制发电厂的排放仍在争论中。法律专家说，周二的裁决没有批准他这样做，但确实给了他尝试的余地。任何新的努力都肯定会受到保守派的挑战，很可能在最高法院面前面临不确定的未来。
 拜登过渡小组的发言人没有立即回应置评请求。
note_en:
WASHINGTON — A federal appeals court on Tuesday struck down the Trump administration’s plan to relax restrictions on greenhouse gas emissions from power plants, paving the way for President-elect Joseph R. Biden Jr. to enact new and stronger restrictions on power plants.
 The United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia  called the Trump administration’s Affordable Clean Energy rule a “fundamental misconstruction” of the nation’s environmental laws, devised through a “tortured series of misreadings” of legal statute.
 On the last full day of the Trump presidency, it effectively ended the Environmental Protection Agency’s efforts to weaken and undermine climate change policies and capped a dismal string of failures in which courts threw out one deregulation after another. Experts have widely described the E.P.A.’s losing streak as  one of the worst legal records of the agency in modern history.
 The appeals court did not reinstate a 2015 regulation that President Barack Obama’s E.P.A. had enacted, which would have forced utilities to move away from coal and toward renewable energy to reduce emissions. But it rejected the Trump administration’s attempt to repeal and replace that rule with a toothless one.
 Judges eviscerated the Trump administration’s core argument: that the only possible way to interpret the Clean Air Act of 1970 is that the federal government does not have the authority to set national restrictions on emissions or force states to move away from fossil fuel power. That argument that would have prevented Mr. Biden or any future administration from tackling climate change from power plants without an explicit new law from Congress.
 The Trump administration, the judges said, “may not shirk its responsibility by imagining new limitations that the plain language of the statute does not clearly require.”
 Molly Block, a spokeswoman for the E.P.A., said the Trump administration was disappointed in the ruling and said it “risks injecting more uncertainty at a time when the nation needs regulatory stability.”
 Environmental groups and legal experts saw the decision as a vindication of the argument that the government does have the authority to tackle climate change.
 “It’s a massive win,” said Jody Freeman, a professor of environmental law at Harvard University who served as an adviser to the Obama administration.
  A core promise of the Biden campaign was to eliminate fossil fuel emissions from the power sector by 2035. With Tuesday’s ruling, the Biden administration will not have to wait for the legal fight over the Trump rule to play out before deciding whether or how to use regulation to tackle climate change, Ms. Freeman said. Instead, she said, the Biden E.P.A. can “go on the offense” immediately.
 “The real win here is that the Trump administration failed to tie the Biden team’s hands,” Ms. Freeman said. “They wanted to lock in a narrow legal interpretation and make it impossible for a new administration to set ambitious standards for power plants. That was their whole strategy. And it went down to spectacular defeat.”
 In 2015 the Obama administration enacted the Clean Power Plan, which aimed to cut emissions from the power sector 32 percent by 2030 compared to 2005 levels. To do so, it instructed every state to draft plans to eliminate carbon emissions from power plants by phasing out coal and increasing the generation of renewable energy.
 The measure never went into effect. The Supreme Court in 2016 said states did not have to comply until a barrage of lawsuits from conservative states and the coal industry had been resolved. Shortly after the election of President Trump, his E.P.A. repealed the Clean Power Plan.
  Andrew Wheeler, the departing administrator of the E.P.A. and a former coal lobbyist, replaced the plan with the weaker Affordable Clean Energy rule, which maintained the law only allows the agency to set guidelines to reduce emissions at individual power plants with actions like increasing efficiency or upgrading boilers that do not threaten an entire power sector, such as coal.
 Alex Flint, executive director of the Alliance for Market Solutions, a conservative group that advocates for a carbon tax, said the ruling created “a mess” for the owners of power plants, steel mills, cement kilns and other polluting industries.
 “One administration pushes rules in one direction, and the next pushes them in the other. Then, a court throws out the rules. It is impossible to make efficient long-term decisions,” he said.
 He called for Congress to put a price on emissions “so that polluters can decide whether to continue operations and pay the cost of doing so or change their operations.”
 Ms. Freeman said the administration could attempt some last-minute filings in the next 24 hours but saw their chances of success as very low.
 Whether Mr. Biden will seek to again use regulation to curb power plant emissions is still being debated. Tuesday’s ruling, legal experts said, did not give him approval to do so, but it did give him the leeway to try. Any new effort would certainly be challenged by conservatives and would very likely face an uncertain future before the Supreme Court.
 A spokesman for the Biden transition team did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
title: 法院宣告特朗普的气候遭受“折磨”
title_en: Court Voids a ‘Tortured’ Trump Climate Rollback

石墨烯是排列成鸡丝状的碳原子晶格，这种结构使其非常适合广泛的应用。
  这项新技术通过快速加热任何碳基材料（例如用过的咖啡渣或塑料废料）来降低成本和难度。
   莱斯大学开发的最新技术正将一个人的垃圾带到另一个极端的想法。香蕉皮，咖啡渣，一次性塑料容器，煤炭-所有这些以及更多这些东西都被变成了周围最有价值的材料之一：石墨烯。化学家James Tour和他的团队开发了一种快速的过程，可以将大量的垃圾转化为石墨烯薄片。
 ＆＃34;这很重要，＆＃34;赖斯在莱斯大学的新闻稿中说。 ＆＃34;世界上有30％到40％的食物被扔掉了，因为它变质了，塑料废物引起了全世界的关注。我们已经证明，任何固体碳基物质，包括混合的塑料废料和橡胶轮胎，都可以转化为石墨烯。
 石墨烯的价值主要归因于其令人难以置信的强度以及它所拥有的广泛的工业应用。这种材料由一层碳原子组成，该碳原子层通过六个化学键相互连接，形成类似于鸡丝的晶格。
 石墨烯不仅因其高反应活性和强度而在科学实验中极为有用，而且还可以添加到其他各种材料中以提高其强度或使其更轻便，例如混凝土或金属。它是导电性最强的材料，因此在LED或智能手机中用作散热器非常有价值。它也可以用在电池技术，油漆，传感器等中，实际上，仅在本文中，这种材料的应用就太多了。
 尽管石墨烯具有很高的实用性，但它还不是我们日常生活的一部分。部分原因是因为其成本过高。石墨烯难以批量生产，目前的石墨烯商业价格为每吨67,000美元至200,000美元，＆＃34;游说。常见的技术包括剥落（其中石墨烯的薄片从石墨上剥离下来）或化学气相沉积法，其中甲烷（CH4）在捕获甲烷碳原子的铜基质存在下汽化，将其排列为石墨烯。 
这项新技术称为快速焦耳加热，它更简单，更便宜，并且不依赖任何有害溶剂或化学添加剂。简而言之，碳基材料会在2760°C（5,000°F）的温度下暴露10毫秒。这会破坏输入材料中的每个化学键。除碳以外的所有原子都会转化为气体，在这种概念验证装置中会逸出，但可以在工业应用中捕获。但是，碳本身会重新组装成石墨烯薄片。
 而且，该技术产生了所谓的静压石墨烯。其他方法产生的是所谓的A-B堆叠石墨烯，其中一片石墨烯中的一半原子位于另一片石墨烯的原子上。这导致两张纸之间的粘合更紧密，从而使它们更难分离。涡轮静压石墨烯在片材之间没有这种顺序，因此它们彼此之间更容易除去。
 研究人员称之为“闪光石墨烯”的最明显的用例。是将这些石墨烯薄片用作混凝土中的组分。 ＆＃34;通过用石墨烯加固混凝土，＆＃34;托尔说，我们可以减少建筑用的混凝土，从而减少制造和运输的成本。从本质上讲，我们是在捕获可能浪费食物的二氧化碳和甲烷等温室气体，这些垃圾会在垃圾填埋场排放。我们正在将这些碳转化为石墨烯，并将该石墨烯添加到混凝土中，从而降低了混凝土生产中产生的二氧化碳量。这是使用石墨烯的双赢环境方案。
 混凝土是这种材料的主要应用，既可以在经济和环境上做到合理，又有许多其他应用。随着这种用于批量生产石墨烯的方法和其他方法的成熟，我们可以希望看到具有越来越强大，更轻巧，更先进且对环境破坏较小的材料和技术的未来。

Graphene is a lattice of carbon atoms arranged in a chicken-wire formation, a structure that makes it very useful for a wide range of applications.
  This new technique cuts down on the cost and difficulty by flash heating any carbon-based material, such as used coffee grounds or plastic waste.
   Recent technology developed at Rice University is taking the idea that one man's trash is another man's treasure to its extreme. Banana peels, coffee grounds, single-use plastic containers, coal — all of these and more are being turned into one of the most valuable materials around: graphene. Chemist James Tour and his team have developed a rapid process that can transform bulk-quantities of junk into flakes of graphene.
 "This is a big deal," said Tour in a Rice University  press release. "The world throws out 30 percent to 40 percent of all food, because it goes bad, and plastic waste is of worldwide concern. We've already proven that any solid carbon-based matter, including mixed plastic waste and rubber tires, can be turned into graphene."
 Graphene's value is mainly due to its incredible strength and the wide variety of industrial applications it possesses. This material consists of a single layer of carbon atoms connected to one another by six chemical bonds, creating a lattice that resembles chicken wire.
 Not only is graphene extremely useful in scientific experiments due to its high reactivity and strength, it can also be added to all sorts of other materials to improve their strength or to make them more lightweight, such as concrete or metals. It is the most conductive material, making it invaluable for use as a heat sink in, for instance, LEDs or smartphones. It could also be used in battery technology, in paints, in sensors, and many more — there are quite literally too many applications for this material to cover in this article alone.
 Despite its high utility, graphene isn't a part of our everyday lives yet. Part of the reason why is because of its prohibitive cost. Graphene is difficult to produce in bulk, with "the present commercial price of graphene being $67,000 to $200,000 per ton," said Tour. Common techniques include exfoliation, in which sheets of graphene are stripped away from graphite, or chemical vapor deposition, in which methane (CH4) is vaporized in the presence of a copper substrate that grabs the methane's carbon atoms, arranging them as graphene.
 The new technique, called  flash Joule heating, is far simpler, cheaper, and doesn't rely on any hazardous solvents or chemical additives. Simply put, a carbon-based material is exposed to a 2,760°C (5,000°F) heat for just 10 milliseconds. This breaks every chemical bond in the input material. All atoms aside from carbon turn into gas, which escape in this proof-of-concept device but could be captured in industrial applications. The carbon, however, reassembles itself as flakes of graphene.
 What's more, this technique produces so-called turbostatic graphene. Other processes produce what's known as A-B stacked graphene, in which half of the atoms in one sheet of graphene lie over the atoms of another sheet of graphene. This results in a tighter bond between the two sheets, making them harder to separate. Turbostatic graphene has no such order between sheets, so they're easier to remove from one another.
 The most obvious use case for what the researchers have termed "flash graphene" is to use these graphene flakes as a component in concrete. "By strengthening concrete with graphene," said Tour, "we could use less concrete for building, and it would cost less to manufacture and less to transport. Essentially, we're trapping greenhouse gases like carbon dioxide and methane that waste food would have emitted in landfills. We are converting those carbons into graphene and adding that graphene to concrete, thereby lowering the amount of carbon dioxide generated in concrete manufacture. It's a win-win environmental scenario using graphene."
 Concrete is a major application for this material, one that would both be economically and environmentally sound, but many others exist too. As this method and others for producing graphene in bulk mature, we can hope to see a future with increasingly stronger, more lightweight, more advanced, and less environmentally destructive materials and technologies.
title: 新技术将垃圾变成有价值的石墨烯
title_en: New technique turns junk into valuable graphene
